coredata.dc:title: Insights into the formation of N doped 3D-graphene quantum dots. Spectroscopic and computational approach
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Algarra, Manuel
Moreno, Virginia
Lázaro-Martínez, Juan M.
Rodríguez-Castellón, Enrique
Soto, Juan
Morales, Julián
Benítez, Almudena
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we utilize a top-down approach to synthesize nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots from a 3D-graphene precursor via an eco-friendly hydrothermal method. The nanoparticles obtained showed a 2–3 nm diameter and well dispersion behavior in aqueous media. The reaction mechanism of insertion of nitrogen from polyvinylpolypyrrolidone onto the 3D-graphene structure, via an esterification reaction, was studied by the density functional theory, in addition, the kinetic and thermodynamic magnitudes of the reaction was analyzed with the help of Eyring's transition state theory and statistical thermodynamics. After analysis by ss-NMR and XPS spectroscopies, the functional groups involved in this process were characterized, and N was found mainly as amide/amine groups. Fluorescence emission, which exhibited a red shift (552 nm) and an emission maximum at 512 nm when excited at 480 nm, demonstrated a low stoke shift (Δλ = 32 nm), explained by the proposed structural model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
XPS
Solid-state NMR
DFT calculations

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reaction mechanism of PVP polymer with 3D-graphene

VM is acknowledged to Erasmus+ (KA103) program. AB thanks the financial support from Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitiveness ( MAT2017-87541-R ) and Andalusian Regional Government (FQM-175 groups). JS thanks Rafael Larrosa and Darío Guerrero for the technical support in running the calculations and the SCBI (Supercomputer and Bioinformatics) center of the University of Málaga for computer resources and the financial support from Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitiveness (Project: CTQ2015-65816-R). ERC thanks to project RTI2018-099668-BC22 of Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades , and project UMA18-FEDERJA-126 of Junta de Andalucía. JMLM thanks MINCyT (Project 095/16), UBA (UBACyT 2017-2019/22BA) and CONICET (PIP 2014-2016/130) from Argentina, and Universidad de Málaga (Spain) for his researcher fellowship. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Survey XPS spectra of 3D-G and N-QDs, (B) C 1s core level spectra for of 3D-G and (C) C 1s core level spectra for of N-GQDs.

(A) 1H ss-NMR (MAS rate: 32 kHz) results for the GO, (B) 3D-G, (C) N-GQD covering with PVP (D) 3D graphene-PVP-NCQD material. (E) 13C DP experiments (MAS rate: 15 kHz) for the GO (F) 3D-G. 13C CP-MAS (MAS rate: 15 kHz) (G) and (H) 13C DP experiments (MAS rate: 15 kHz) for 3D graphene-PVP-NCQD material. The numbering corresponds to those in Scheme 1. The spinning-side bands are marked with an asterisk.
Molecular geometries of the reactants, transition states and products of the reaction of the monomeric unit of PVPP with different models of graphene oxides: (A) C1; (B) C2; (C) C3.
Potential energy profiles (interpolation lines) for association/dissociation of the catalyst to (A) C1; (B) C2; (C) C3. CAM-B3LYP/def2-TZVPP.
Normalized fluorescence spectra of the N-GCDs aqueous solution under excitation with different wavelengths ranged between 400 and 480 nm.
GO representative chemical structure and chemical reaction between PVP and 3D graphene. For the PVP-3D graphene the indicated 13C chemical shift corresponds to NMR results in Fig. 3.
Proposed hydroxylic positions of esterification reaction.
CAM-B3LYP/def2-TZVPP adsorption energies (enthalpy, kcal/mol), reaction rates (k, s−1) and Arrhenius activation energy (kcal/mol) at T = 298.15 and 453.15 K. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Label of the geometries according to Scheme 2.

head.ce:title: Insights into the formation of N doped 3D-graphene quantum dots. Spectroscopic and computational approach

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we utilize a top-down approach to synthesize nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots from a 3D-graphene precursor via an eco-friendly hydrothermal method. The nanoparticles obtained showed a 2–3 nm diameter and well dispersion behavior in aqueous media. The reaction mechanism of insertion of nitrogen from polyvinylpolypyrrolidone onto the 3D-graphene structure, via an esterification reaction, was studied by the density functional theory, in addition, the kinetic and thermodynamic magnitudes of the reaction was analyzed with the help of Eyring's transition state theory and statistical thermodynamics. After analysis by ss-NMR and XPS spectroscopies, the functional groups involved in this process were characterized, and N was found mainly as amide/amine groups. Fluorescence emission, which exhibited a red shift (552 nm) and an emission maximum at 512 nm when excited at 480 nm, demonstrated a low stoke shift (Δλ = 32 nm), explained by the proposed structural model.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-based materials are gaining increasing interest because of their excellent chemical, physical and mechanical properties that provide the opportunity to be used in a wide variety of applications in the field of research. Some of the most important properties exhibited by this light material are high thermal and electrical conductivity, excellent hardness, strength, elasticity, flexibility, electronic and optical properties [1–4]. Graphene, a 2D sheet of sp2 hybridized carbon, can chemically modify its sheets by integrating into three-dimensional macroporous structures through various gelation techniques to form 3D networks of graphene-based gels, which could be called 3D-graphene (3D-G) [5]. 3D-G materials can be prepared from graphitic oxide (GO) by different methods, including hydrothermal reduction [6], chemical reduction [7] and template-directed method [8]. Also, chemical vapor deposition can be used to obtain 3D-graphene [9].

Besides, special attention is being paid to the structural conversion of graphene into graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [10], which differ of the well-known carbon dots (CDs) with graphene lattice inside the dots, with a mean size <100 nm and <10 layers thick [11]. GQDs therefore show a large diameter and surface area, grafted by using a conjugated π-π network and other physical properties, such as highly tunable photoluminescence, remarkable UV-blocking, good biocompatibility, and high photo stability. The fields of applications are widely present in chemical sensing [12–17], drug delivery [18–20], bio-imaging [21–23], photocatalysis [24,25], photo detectors [26,27], electrochemical luminescence [28,29], optical sensors [30,31] and photovoltaic devices [32,33], just to mention some of them.

As mentioned above, GQDs present a unique photoluminescence behavior in comparison with to the homologous CDs [34], given that the modulation of the emission can be tuned by doping the material with heteroatoms, an effective approach to improve the activity of GQDs. Insertion of N atomsis the most widely studied method due to the similar atomic size between carbon and nitrogen atoms [35,36], which makes easy to incorporate heteroatoms into the material and improve the physical chemistry properties of the graphene systems. Previous bottom-up or high temperature (1000 °C) approaches have obtained similar properties for synthesis of heteroatom-doped graphene QDs derivates, in order to be applied to hydrogen, lithium or sodium storage [37,38], electronic devices [39] and batteries [40].

In this work, the preparation of 3D-G was carried out by simple microwave-assisted solvothermal pathway and further doped with N atoms by a hydrothermal approach. It was investigated by different spectroscopic analyses to obtain the information about how N is inserted onto the 3D-G structure, in order to explain the red shifted found. In addition, the reaction mechanism was studied by means of quantum chemical calculations.

In a first stage, graphite oxide precursor (GO) was synthesized from pristine graphite flakes (Merck) by a modified Hummers method [41]. In a second stage, a 3D-G was obtained by exfoliating and reducing GO through a solvothermal treatment assisted by microwave radiation. In detail, GO (80 mg) was dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water by ultrasonication for 30 min. Subsequently, the resulting dispersion was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to microwave radiation in an oven (Milestone flexiWAVE) at a power of 350 W and a temperature of 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling the autoclave at room temperature, the hydrogel in the form of a monolith was filtered and washed several times with distilled water to remove the residual salts. Then, the wet hydrogel was cooled at −70 °C and freeze-dried (Telstar Lyo Quest, Mod. 85) until 50 °C to obtain the 3D-G like a sponge [42].

N-doped graphene carbon nanoparticles were prepared by mixing 3D-G (50 mg) and polyvinylpolypyrrolidone (PVP) (Sigma-Aldrich, average mol. Wt. 40000, 80 mg), by a hydrothermal approach at 180 °C for 2 h. Both reactants were mixed in H2O (2 mL) and transferred to a Teflon reactor lined stainless steel. After that, the obtained solution could stand at room temperature, obtaining a homogeneous solution which, after a filtration step in order to remove the unreacted starting materials, a fluorescent material was obtained under ultraviolet light, which marked the investigation as N-GQDs.
The morphology of N-GQDs was analysed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and examined under a FEI Talos F200X.

XPS were carried out on a Physical Electronics PHI VersaProbe II spectrometer using monochromatic Al-Kα radiation (49.1 W, 15 kV and 1486.6 eV). C 1s, O 1s and N 1s core-level spectra were analyzed. They were recorded with a constant pass energy value of 29.35 eV, using a 100 μm diameter circular analysis area, and analyzed using PHI SmartSoft software and processed using MultiPak 9.3 package. Shirley-type background and Gauss-Lorentz curves were used to determine the binding energies. The binding energy values were referenced to adventitious carbon C 1s signal (284.8 eV).
Fluorescence measurements were performed using 10-mm path-length quartz cuvettes on a fluorescence spectrometer LS55 (Perkin-Elmer) at room temperature.

The ss-NMR experiment data were acquired with a Bruker Avance-III HD spectrometer equipped with a 14.1 T narrow bore magnet operating at Larmor frequencies of 600.09 MHz and 150.91 MHz for 1H and 13C, respectively. Powdered samples were packed into 3.2 or 2.5 mm ZrO2 rotors and rotated at room temperature at magic angle spinning (MAS) rates of 15 or 35 kHz, respectively. 13C-CP-MAS (cross-polarization and magic angle spinning) experiments were done in a 3.2 mm MAS probe. Glycine was used as external reference compound for the recording of the 13C spectra and to set the Hartmann-Hahn matching condition in the CP-MAS experiments in 13C spectra. The contact time during CP was 2000 µs. The SPINAL64 sequence (small phase incremental alternation with 64 steps) was used for heteronuclear decoupling during acquisition. 13C natural abundance direct polarization experiments (13C DP) with proton decoupling (SPINAL64) during acquisition was conducted [43]. An excitation pulse of 4.0 μs and a recycling time of 100 s was used., and 4000 scans were accumulated in order to obtain quantitative and good signal-to-noise ratio. 1H-MAS ss-NMR spectra were recorded in a 2.5 mm MAS probe using single pulse excitation experiments. Chemical shifts for 1H (in ppm) are relative to (CH3)4Si.

In order to provide additional insights into the mechanism of the catalytic reaction of PVP polymer with 3D-G), such a reaction have been theoretically studied by means of density functional theory (DFT) by applying the hybrid exchange-correlation functional CAM-B3LYP [44], in conjunction with the def2-TZVPP basis sets for all of the elements [45,46]. Each stationary point on the potential energy surfaces has been characterized by computing the respective analytical harmonic frequencies. The products and reactants connected by each transition state can be unambiguously localized by means of an intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC) [47,48] calculation. Geometrical optimizations of minima and transition states were carried out in redundant internal coordinates without applying geometrical constraints; therefore, the results are independent of the any choice of the internal variables. All the electronic structure calculations were carried out with the program GAUSSIAN16 [49]. Adsorption energies and activation energies were determined by using Transition State Theory and Thermodynamics Statistical with programs implemented by our research group [50,51]. The potential energy surfaces which lead to the formation of N-GQDs complexes have been studied by means of the linear interpolation method [52–58]. Molecular geometries were analyzed with the help of the programs: MacMolPlt [59] and MOLDEN [60].

The preparation of N-GQDs was performed in a Teflon reactor by one step reaction. The selected conditions for the synthesis of N-GQDs were optimized as further discussed below. The first parameter was the reaction temperature. In this case, temperatures between 150 and 250 °C were tested. The best results were achieved at 180 °C, and this temperature was selected for further experiments. Reaction time between 1 and 3 h was another parameter to optimize, obtaining the best condition using 2 h of reaction time. Several PVP/3D-G molar ratios (0.16, 0.64, 1.6, 6.4, 16) were tested, according with the fluorescence obtained when mixed, finding 1.6 as the optimal ratio and maximal fluorescence intensity. When molar ratios were bigger than the optimal one, a fluorescence was observed in the synthesized product. In contrast, for those molar ratios lower than 1.6, no fluorescence was observed due to the reaction was not completed. For this reason, all further experiments were performed with 1.6 as a molar ratio.

The morphology of the hydrothermal synthesized N-GQDs was analyzed by TEM microscopy (Fig. 1 ). The N-GQDs appear spherical and well dispersed, with a diameter, estimated from TEM images, are mainly distributed in the narrow range of 2–3 nm, in concordance with the zeta potential obtained (-14.5 ± 0.45 mV), indicates a moderate dispersion and stability.

With PVP/3D-G molar ratios of 1.6, the obtained N-GQDs exhibit high solubility and stability in water due to the introduction of the nitrogen/oxygen-containing functional groups. To investigate the surface of N-GQDs, XPS analysis was performed.

In order to analyze the surface of N-GQDs, XPS spectra were recorded (Fig. 2 ). The survey scans from the XPS for N-GQDs shows the insertion of the N atoms onto the 3D-G structure at 400.0 eV (Fig. 2A). The surface chemical composition (at %), after the hydrothermal process, changed to: C (72%), O (14.7%) and N (11.5%) from C (83.6%) and O (16.7%) for 3D-G. The C 1s core level spectrum of 3D-G is very complex (Fig. 2B) and can be decomposed in several contributions, as previously was discussed [61]. Otherwise, the C 1s spectrum for N-GQDs can be deconvoluted in three contributions assigned to the main for CC/CH (285.1 eV, 60.56%) and CO/CN/COC (acetal) (286.1 eV, 20.38%), and a last one with an intensity (19.06%) at 287.6 eV assigned to carboxylate [62] (Fig. 2C).

N 1s contribution is only a strong peak at 399.7 eV, assigned to amine/amide functional groups NH2/OCNH2 [63] (Scheme 1 , Fig. S1A). Moreover, The O 1s XPS spectrum is decomposed in two contributions (Fig. S1B), at 531.2 (81%) and 532.4 eV (19%), which can be attributed to CO and carboxylate groups the main contribution, and to COH and COC groups the latter one, which demonstrated the presence of the ester onto the surface of N-GQDs [64]. FTIR spectra show two strong bands at 1641 and 1240 cm−1 which clearly correspond to carbonyl and acetate functionals groups respectively. Bands at 1424 and 3500 cm−1 correspond to CH2 and NH groups (Fig. SI. 2A). By Raman spectroscopy, it is not possible to detect the D and G bands, characteristic of graphenoide structures, explained by the surface incorporation of PVP (Fig. SI. 2B), the main bands mentioned in FTIR such as the acetate bond (940 cm−1), CH2 (1428 cm−1) and clearly the band at 1662 cm−1 assigned to amide groups (OCNH) The strong band at 2930 cm−1 is attributed to CH2 groups.

In order to retrieve information regarding the chemical reaction between PVP and 3D-G graphene, both 1H and 13C ss-NMR experiments were done and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . First, the 1H-MAS spectrum for the graphene oxide shows a broad NMR line with a major component at 6.3 ppm assigned to the sp 2 hydrogens of the graphitic structure. Moreover, some other proton populations are observed at around 4 and 2 ppm corresponding to oxygenated methyne (CH OH and CH O) and methylene (CH2
) hydrogens, respectively. Concerning the 13C spectra, the excitation pulses in the 13C CP-MAS experiments perturbed the rotation at the magic angle spinning (MAS) rates for the graphene oxide or 3D graphene. For this reason, the 13C ss-NMR experiment were studied by direct polarization experiments in order to get stability of the rotors under the MAS rates and radiofrequency pulse conditions for GO and 3D-G. The 13C DP-NMR spectrum for GO presents two carbon populations associated with sp 3 and sp 2 carbons at 50–80 and 110–150 ppm, respectively. Regarding the sp 3 region, the presence of both oxygenated aliphatic chains (70 ppm) and oxirane groups (59 ppm) is evidenced, while the sp 2 region shows the characteristic signal of graphitic carbons (CC, 130 ppm) commonly found in this kind of materials [65–68].

When 3D-G is prepared from the GO, a new material is obtained according to the NMR results which it remains the graphitic signal at 128 ppm as the main signal together with minor signals associated with oxygenated ( CHOH) and methylene carbons ( CH2 ) as indicated in Fig. 3F. Although the spectrum of 13C DP-MAS was acquired for a week compared to the spectrum of the GO that was acquired in 12 h, the relaxation in these materials is strongly affected, which is associated to flaws or gaps that these materials possess that enhance the relaxation, avoiding their correct visualization. The 1H-MAS spectrum also indicates that the oxygenated content was dramatically reduced at 3–5 ppm with an NMR line that it is practically composed mainly with the graphitic hydrogens at 6.7 ppm and a minor contribution of methyl groups at 2 ppm. The 1H-MAS ss-NMR spectra of the N-GCQDs only shows a broad signal at 4.5–0 ppm concerning the protons of the hydrocarbon chain of the unburned polymeric structure of the PVP that covers the N-GCDs nanomaterial together with a prominent free water signal at 5.1 ppm [69]. On the other hand, the 1H-MAS spectrum for the thermal reaction between 3D-G and N-GCDs shows an aromatic component at higher frequency values than in the pure N-GQDs, which demonstrates the inclusion of the sp 2 component into the polymeric material. In considering that 1H-MAS ss-NMR experiment cannot prove the chemical reaction or physical mixture among these materials due to the poor 1H resolution obtained, the 13C CP-MAS were conducted.

The chemical reaction between graphene and PVP polymer was previously reported [70]. However, the present work is the first report that confirm through solid-state NMR experiments that the chemical reaction took place. The 13C ss-NMR spectrum for the PVP-3D-G shows that PVP polymer signals were well resolved together with three new signals at 160.4, 117.8 and 81.8 ppm assigned to the ester [71], graphitic and oxygenated carbons, respectively (Scheme 1). These three signals demonstrated that the nucleophilic attack of the hydroxyl groups in 3D-G to the carbonyl carbon of the amide group in the PVP structure gave rise to the chemical bounding of the graphitic structure to the polymeric material that also contains the N-GQDs. Even when the signal to noise ratio of the 3D-G moieties is significantly lower in comparison with the PVP structure in the CP-MAS experiment, the 13C DP spectrum allowed to obtain a better resolution of the entire chemical composition and without spinning-side bands. The enhance of the relaxation of the inherent delocalized electronic graphitic structure as well experimental issues related with the spinning and probe tuning problems [68,70] with graphitic/graphene materials may avoid the correct visualization of 13C NMR signals, however, we found that the carbon resonance signals of importance were observed with a long acquisition time and an appropriate delay time from both CP-MAS and DP experiments.

Given that it is hypothesized that the reaction consists on an esterification, the molecular models that have been chosen to represent the catalyst consists on substituted benzopyrenes with an hydroxylic group bonded at different positions (Scheme 2 ), in addition, we have considered only the monomeric unit of the PVP polymer in accordance with a recent published article which deals with the binding affinity of the PVP polymer towards phenolic compounds [72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction mechanism of PVP polymer with 3D-graphene
ce:section.ce:para: PVP polymer can attack the catalyst from different directions, which would lead to multiple adsorption complexes. In order to simplify our study, we have followed a strategy based on the reaction product under consideration (esterification product). To be specific, the first step of our studies on the reaction mechanisms consisted in localizing the transition states on the potential energy surface for each selected molecular model. Once the transition states were identified on the respective surfaces, an IRC calculation was carried out in both the reverse and forward direction of the eigenvector associated to the imaginary frequency of the transition state (Fig. S2A-C). At the prefixed end points of the IRC, that is, thirty steps along both directions, a minimum geometry optimization was performed. In consequence, each adsorption complex of the reactant in this work is referred to the minimum geometry optimized after such IRC analysis.

Fig. 4 represents the critical points (reactants, transition states and products) of the esterification reaction for the three models selected in this study. The calculations demonstrate that adsorption of PVP occurs through formation of a hydrogen bond, which arises from the interaction of the oxygen atom of the carboxylic group of the monomer with a hydrogen atom of the hydroxylic moiety of the catalyst. Table 1 collects the adsorption enthalpies (ΔadH) of the three complexes calculated in this work in accordance along with the first-order rate constants and Arrhenius activation energies at different temperatures (T = 298.15 and 453.15 K).

In accordance with the values presented in Table 1, the most efficient complex for esterification of the pyrrolidone polymer is the geometry labeled as C3 in Fig. 4, whose hydroxylic substituent is placed at the border of the carbon shell. It is worthy to note that increasing the temperature from ambient temperature to 455.15 K (180 °C), the rate constants increases ~12 orders of magnitude.

It is interesting to analyze how the potential energy of the PVP:3D-G complex changes as the adsorbate approaches to the active site. Therefore, in order to achieve a picture of the energetic of the adsorption process, a linear interpolation [51–56] from the dissociate reactants to the complex formation was performed. Fig. 5 represents the energy profiles of the formation of the three complexes depicted in Fig. 4. Such interpolations show that the potential surfaces for the formation of the three complexes are barrierless, which implies that the catalyst acts as an attractor. In other words, the adsorbate does not find the active site of the catalyst randomly but is directed to it by the forces that arise from the topology of the potential energy surfaces (Fig. SI. 3).

Further insights were found after analysis of the emission spectrum of N-GQDs. Fig. 6 shows the normalized steady state fluorescence spectra for N-GQDs irradiated with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 400 to 480 nm. It is observed the tunable behavior of the material that changes gradually from 474 to 552 nm (red shifted), a typical effect of CDs with a maximum emission at 512 nm. If we compare the obtained N doped carbon nanoparticles when PVP is used to obtain carbon nanoparticles [63], the maximum is around 495 nm, where here is observed the influence of the 3D-G moiety in this observed Stokes shift. This is accompanied by the decrease of the fluorescence peak intensity, which means that N-GQDs show an excitation dependent emission. Generally, such a variable emission has been attributed to “defect states” with a broad energy distribution on either graphitized core of N-GQDs or the oxygenated and nitrogenated groups that are commonly present on the surface (carbonyl and amide/amine), as demonstrated previously with the XPS analysis and supported by the theoretical calculations.

In these fluorescence spectra, it was noted a small Stokes shift (Δλ = 32 nm, 38.7 eV), which can be explained on the basis of the greater intermolecular interactions, as it was showed in Fig. 4A-C. Molecules having planar structure, in our case the PVP inserted onto N-GQDs, give maximum stokes shift due to greater intermolecular interactions as compared to non-planar molecules. Molecules having rotatable functional groups tends to attain planarity which increase their conjugation also and therefore, causing the obtained red shift. Molecules having donor-acceptor architecture are even more prone to severe intermolecular interactions. Therefore, the PVP molecule according to the observed results is inserted onto the 3D-G structure and can rotate to acquire a planar conformation, which in turn, affect their electronic properties.

In summary, a hydrothermal method was used to synthesize a highly soluble N-GQDs from 3D-Graphene. TEM images showed well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with 2–3 nm of mean diameter. XPS and NMR analysis demonstrated that the insertion of PVP, as nitrogen source for doping 3D-Graphene is obtained by an esterification process. The fluorescence spectra demonstrated the excitation wavelength dependence, which explain the different functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs. In addition, the esterification reaction mechanism of PVP with 3D-graphene was studied with DFT and the kinetic and thermodynamic magnitudes of the reaction was analyzed with the help of Eyring's transition state theory and statistical thermodynamics.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: MA thanks to ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação, through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005 - Centro de Química da Madeira - CQM+ (Madeira 14-20). VM is acknowledged to Erasmus+ (KA103) program. AB thanks the financial support from Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitiveness (MAT2017-87541-R) and Andalusian Regional Government (FQM-175 groups). JS thanks Rafael Larrosa and Darío Guerrero for the technical support in running the calculations and the SCBI (Supercomputer and Bioinformatics) center of the University of Málaga for computer resources and the financial support from Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitiveness (Project: CTQ2015-65816-R). ERC thanks to project RTI2018-099668-BC22 of Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades, and project UMA18-FEDERJA-126 of Junta de Andalucía. JMLM thanks MINCyT (Project 095/16), UBA (UBACyT 2017-2019/22BA) and CONICET (PIP 2014-2016/130) from Argentina, and Universidad de Málaga (Spain) for his researcher fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.11.044.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Honeycomb carbon: a review of graphene
